retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Compression -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Approaches
retrieved_docs.content: As shown above, a wide variety of approaches to the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine are available in the armamentarium of the spine surgeon. Approaches should be selected based on the location of the pathology in the spine and its relation to the spinal canal. In addition, the surgeon should always consider his or her skill level and what is safest in the surgeon's hands. The patient's preexisting medical conditions also play a large part in selecting an approach. The best approach is often the safest approach.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Myelopathy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of DCM must be individualized according to the severity of myelopathy, imaging findings, and patient factors. Surgical decompression is strongly indicated for moderate and severe DCM to maximize neurological recovery.

retrieved_docs.content: The choice between anterior and posterior decompression depends on the anatomic extent and location of the pathology, with both approaches yielding comparable long-term outcomes when applied to suitable candidates.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural tumors and cervical spondylosis myelopathy can be effectively ruled out by a spine MRI and the absence of classic findings found in SCD of the spinal cord.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Myelopathy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hybrid approaches—combining corpectomy at 1 level with discectomy and fusion at adjacent levels—or skip corpectomies can tailor the decompression to irregular patterns of pathology while preserving intervening vertebral bodies, potentially reducing graft stress. However, these techniques are technically demanding, carry similar risks of graft failure, and usually prolong operative time. The role of cervical arthroplasty in established cervical myelopathy remains somewhat debated because fusion reliably immobilizes the affected segment, thereby minimizing any dynamic component of cord compression. However, when myelopathy is caused solely by a focal, noncalcified disc herniation (without significant osteophytes, OPLL, or segmental kyphosis), disc replacement is broadly accepted as a valid, ‘relative’ indication without major controversy.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Myelopathy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The timeliness of intervention is critical. Patients with milder myelopathy and shorter preoperative durations of symptoms tend to have improved outcomes after surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Compression -- Treatment / Management -- General Management
retrieved_docs.content: In a 2014 retrospective study of this practice, 73 out of 142 patients with SEA were successfully treated with an antibiotic-alone approach. Statistically significant factors associated with failure of non-operative management included age of more than 65 years, diabetes, MRSA infection, neurologic compromise, and circumferential SEA. [28] The decision to pursue non-operative management should only be made with direct neurosurgical consultation for risk factor assessment and admission and with the individual patient's preferences in mind.

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Compression -- Treatment / Management -- General Management
retrieved_docs.content: Prompt surgical decompression with laminectomy and evacuation is indicated for SEH with severe or progressive neurologic deficits. Although an exact time interval for the prevention of irreversible neurologic deficits has not been defined, most experts advise evacuation within 48 hours of symptom onset. The role of anticoagulation reversal in non-operative management has been explored in case reports. Still, it is not currently considered standard care for patients with SEH, particularly when neurologic deficits and bowel or bladder dysfunction are present.

retrieved_docs.title: Decompression Sickness -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Experts have classified the manifestations of this disorder into 3 types. Type I DCS presents with skin, musculoskeletal, or lymphatic involvement. Type II DCS manifests with symptoms of brain or spinal injury. Type III DCS presents with potentially fatal pulmonary complications. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of Types I and II DCS and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with these conditions.

This delineation clarifies that such laminectomy techniques are not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. An orbit can be explored via a transcranial approach with lesion removal, which is likewise categorized as an exploration method. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are specified for one or two lumbar vertebral segments (e.g., for spinal stenosis) and are noted as possibly included methods. Additional laminectomy variants cover more extensive cervical or lumbar segments (more than two vertebral levels) and are also listed as possibly included methods of exploration. Spinal exploration procedures are detailed by vertebral level and extent. In addition, Excision is linked to therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as to percutaneous nephrolithotomy, reflecting its applicability to both head‑and‑neck and urologic interventions. Conversely, certain extensive laminectomy procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. These excluded methods include laminectomy with spinal cord or cauda equina exploration/decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis when performed over one or two vertebral segments in the cervical or lumbar spine (except for spondylolisthesis), as well as laminectomy spanning more than two vertebral segments in either the cervical or lumbar regions. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly inspect, access, or remove pathology from specific anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland surgery such as adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Biliary exploration is represented by several cholecystectomy variants that incorporate common duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, each designed to evaluate and treat ductal stones or strictures. Cranial exploration is covered by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy procedures, which allow direct visualization of intracranial lesions. Orbital pathology can be addressed through a transcranial approach to explore the orbit and excise lesions. Spinal exploration procedures include laminectomies—cervical, sacral, lumbar, thoracic, or multilevel—performed without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina in conditions such as spinal stenosis; these may be listed as core methods or as possibly included techniques depending on the extent of vertebral involvement. A related laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) technique provides nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated disc material at a single lumbar level, often as a re‑exploration. Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach exemplifies neck and mediastinal access for hyperparathyroidism. All of these specific methods fall under the broader “Exploration procedure” classification, which itself is organized under the parent category “Clinical actions A‑G.” This hierarchical structure clarifies how diverse surgical explorations are grouped for clinical documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

Spinal exploration procedures encompass a range of laminectomies with exploration and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. Single‑segment cervical laminectomy is a defined method, whereas multi‑segment cervical, lumbar, and thoracic laminectomies are noted as possibly included methods within the exploration category.
